content:
5 min read
Just now

--

Keep public pages and browser tools available through an origin outage while writes fail honestly and recovery happens automatically.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

An origin-first Worker passes healthy responses through and serves a matching static snapshot only when the host is unavailable.A six-hour server-maintenance window does not necessarily mean six hours of continuous downtime. It does mean the exact interruption is unknown, and a manual response can add avoidable minutes before and after the real outage.
Our shared-hosting provider offered a sensible fallback: place a maintenance page on another server, change the DNS record when maintenance starts, and restore it afterward. That would preserve a message, but every page and browser tool would become the same notice.
We wanted a narrower degraded mode. Public guides and client-side tools should remain useful, dynamic actions should fail honestly, and normal hosting should resume without someone staying awake to flip a switch.
This article condenses our implementation. The complete evidence timeline, decision tables, code, QA measurements, and references are available in the original KeyboardTester.click case study.
Origin first, snapshot second
The design places an always-on Cloudflare Worker on the normal domain. Every request first goes to the real shared-hosting origin with a bounded timeout.
If the origin responds normally, the Worker returns that response unchanged. Redirects and legitimate 4xx responses also pass through; they are not evidence of an infrastructure outage.
If the origin times out, cannot be reached, or returns one of a limited set of infrastructure failures, the Worker looks for the same path in a static snapshot. Known GET and HEAD paths can continue as stored pages and assets.
The next request tries the origin again. As soon as the host responds healthily, normal service returns automatically. There is no maintenance-mode switch to forget.
This is a continuity layer, not a second application stack. Forms, database writes, email, server-side AI, saved actions, sessions, and control-panel functions remain unavailable while the origin is down.
Why we captured rendered production
The local PHP workspace contained unpublished changes, so it was not a safe source for the emergency copy. We crawled the rendered canonical site instead.
The resulting package included 1,514 pages and 1,234 referenced first-party assets. With manifests and support files, it contained 2,749 files totaling about 547.6 MB.
The crawler retained canonical links, hreflang, structured data, internal links, styles, scripts, images, fonts, and the assets required by browser-only tools. It stripped service-worker registration and production trackers from the stored copy, then added a restrained continuity notice.
That separation matters. Private staging can stay authenticated, noindex, ad-free, and analytics-free. The production Worker can add the site's delayed analytics and advertising only when it serves fallback HTML.
The routing mistake that can loop forever
Suppose a Worker route covers the public hostname and the Worker fetches that same routed hostname. The request can enter the Worker again instead of reaching shared hosting.
Cloudflare’s resolveOverride can use a controlled DNS-only hostname in the same zone for origin resolution while preserving the public request URL and canonical Host header. This avoids the loop without creating a second public website.
The real bypass hostname, origin address, Cloudflare identifiers, and credentials should never appear in a public tutorial. Verify direct-origin TLS and virtual-host behavior privately.
Preserve honest HTTP behavior
A fallback should not convert every problem into a successful homepage.
Our contract distinguishes four cases:

• Healthy responses, redirects, 4xx responses, and statuses outside the fallback set pass through unchanged.

• A network error or timeout lets a known safe path use the snapshot.

• Selected 5xx and Cloudflare 520–527 responses can trigger the same snapshot lookup.

• Unknown pages and unavailable write actions receive a controlled 503, including Retry-After and Cache-Control: no-store.
This makes the degraded state useful without claiming that a form submission, login, or database update succeeded when it did not.
Test three different systems
Testing only the static homepage is not enough. We treated these as three separate release gates.
First, forced snapshot mode had to preserve the URL, canonical, H1, structured data, assets, notice, localized content, Arabic RTL, mobile layout, HEAD behavior, missing-page response, and write-action 503.
Second, an authenticated origin preview had to prove that the alternate resolution reached the real host without recursion.
Third, healthy public pass-through had to prove the opposite: no fallback marker, unchanged origin pages, preserved redirects, and normal origin-generated behavior.
The remote snapshot audit checked 2,748 page and asset paths without a failure. We forced the failure in controlled staging rather than deliberately breaking the public origin.
Make rollback smaller than deployment
The emergency rollback is route removal. It should not require deleting the Worker, changing DNS, or rebuilding the snapshot.
Our route manager lists existing routes, refuses collisions, adds apex and www independently, verifies each change, and removes only the exact routes owned by the continuity layer.
The routes also fail open if the free Worker request allowance is exhausted. In that situation, requests bypass the Worker and go directly to the hosting origin. This is reasonable because the Worker improves availability; it is not an authentication or security boundary.
Fail-open would be the wrong choice if bypassing the Worker could expose protected content or disable a required security control.
What users and crawlers see
Known read-only pages remain at their original URLs with their existing canonical tags, schema, hreflang, and internal links. They return 200 because the requested content genuinely exists in the snapshot.
Unavailable dynamic actions and unknown paths return 503. Private staging remains blocked from indexing. There is no duplicate public backup domain and no new fallback URL inventory to submit to search engines.
For analytics and advertising, production fallback HTML can receive the site’s delayed integrations through streaming HTML transformation. Healthy origin responses, HEAD, CSS, JavaScript, and images remain untouched. This preserves outage measurement and makes ad delivery possible, but it cannot guarantee impressions or revenue.
Where the pattern fits
This pattern works well for content-heavy sites and browser tools whose essential JavaScript and assets can be captured. It reduces the visible outage bracket without introducing database synchronization or a manual DNS cutover.
It is not enough for a write-heavy SaaS product, ecommerce checkout, account system, or frequently changing application database. Those need a replicated application and data-failover design.
The practical lesson is to match the continuity layer to the failure mode. Keep it small, test failure and recovery separately, expose limitations honestly, and ensure rollback changes less infrastructure than the original deployment.
Read the full implementation, evidence, sanitized examples, and verification checklist at the original source:
https://keyboardtester.click/blog/cloudflare-workers-shared-hosting-failover.php
